audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Judges / Shoftim Deborah-Barak-Jael prose narrative, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Deborah as prophet/judge, Barak's summons, Sisera's chariots, Jael/Heber Kenite setting, Kishon battle, Sisera's flight, Jael's hospitality and killing, and defeat of Jabin.

verses.restored: Children of Israel again did evil in eyes of YHWH after Ehud died.
YHWH sold them into hand of Jabin king of Canaan, who reigned in Hazor; commander of his army was Sisera, and he dwelt in Harosheth-hagoyim.
Children of Israel cried out to YHWH, for he had nine hundred iron chariots, and he oppressed children of Israel with force twenty years.
Deborah, a woman, a prophetess, wife/woman of Lappidoth, she was judging Israel at that time.
She was sitting under Palm of Deborah between Ramah and Bethel in hill country of Ephraim, and children of Israel went up to her for judgment.

She sent and called Barak son of Abinoam from Kedesh-naphtali and said to him, Has not YHWH Divine of Israel commanded? Go and draw toward Mount Tabor, and take with you ten thousand men from sons of Naphtali and from sons of Zebulun.
I will draw to you, to wadi Kishon, Sisera commander of Jabin's army, his chariots and his multitude, and I will give him into your hand.
Barak said to her, If you go with me, I will go; but if you do not go with me, I will not go.

She said, I will surely go with you. Nevertheless the road on which you are going shall not be for your glory, for into hand of a woman YHWH will sell Sisera. Deborah arose and went with Barak to Kedesh.
Barak called Zebulun and Naphtali to Kedesh; ten thousand men went up at his feet, and Deborah went up with him.
Now Heber the Kenite had separated from Kenite, from children of Hobab father-in-law of Moses, and had pitched his tent as far as oak of Zaanannim, which is near Kedesh.
They told Sisera that Barak son of Abinoam had gone up to Mount Tabor.
Sisera called all his chariots, nine hundred chariots of iron, and all people who were with him, from Harosheth-hagoyim to wadi Kishon.
Deborah said to Barak, Rise, for this is day YHWH has given Sisera into your hand. Has not YHWH gone out before you? Barak went down from Mount Tabor, and ten thousand men after him.
YHWH threw Sisera into panic, and all chariots and all camp, by edge of sword before Barak. Sisera went down from chariot and fled on foot.
Barak pursued after chariots and after camp as far as Harosheth-hagoyim, and all camp of Sisera fell by edge of sword; not even one remained.
But Sisera fled on foot to tent of Jael wife of Heber the Kenite, for peace was between Jabin king of Hazor and house of Heber the Kenite.
Jael went out to meet Sisera and said to him, Turn aside, my lord, turn aside to me; do not fear. He turned aside to her into tent, and she covered him with rug/covering.
He said to her, Give me, please, a little water to drink, for I am thirsty. She opened skin of milk, gave him drink, and covered him.
He said to her, Stand at entrance of tent. If a man comes and asks you, saying, Is there a man here? then you shall say, No.
Jael wife of Heber took tent peg and took hammer in her hand, came to him quietly, and drove peg through his temple, and it went down into ground; he was deeply asleep and weary, and he died.
Look, Barak was pursuing Sisera, and Jael went out to meet him and said to him, Go, and I will show you man whom you seek. He came to her, and look, Sisera fallen dead, with peg in his temple.
On that day Divine subdued Jabin king of Canaan before children of Israel.
Hand of children of Israel went on, going harder against Jabin king of Canaan, until they cut off Jabin king of Canaan.

translation_notes: Deborah is explicitly prophetess and judge, publicly accessible under her palm. Her authority is not derivative of Barak and should not be minimized.
Eshet Lappidoth may mean wife of Lappidoth or woman of torches/flames; the phrase requires lexical and reception-history audit.
Barak's refusal to go without Deborah can be read as dependence on prophetic presence rather than simple cowardice; the narrative nevertheless shifts glory to a woman.
The Kenite setting is politically complex: Heber is linked to Moses' in-law people but at peace with Jabin's house, while Jael acts against Sisera inside her tent.

Jael's killing of Sisera uses hospitality, deception, and intimate domestic tools. It may be understood as resistance to an oppressor, but must not be generalized into approval of murder or domestic violence.
Sisera commands a system that oppressed Israel violently for twenty years, yet ethical reception still must resist glamorizing graphic killing.
The prose narrative prepares the poetic celebration in Judges 5, where war language becomes more vivid and morally harder.
safety_note: Do not use Judges 4 to deny women's spiritual authority, shame leaders who rely on prophetic partners, glorify assassination, or romanticize violence inside a home. Center Deborah's public leadership, the cry against oppression, Jael's agency under dangerous political conditions, and the refusal to turn liberation violence into a universal moral pattern.
